Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

DIMENSION: regressions introduced by the 22 pass-1 fixes. Scrutinize the pass-1 changes for new defects: scrubbed_env overwrite-to-empty breaking legitimate CLI operation or wiping a needed var; run_json deadline/seed/parse-only-reroll edge cases; the provenance lock causing contention/deadlock or breaking determinism; pg-pool close-on-failure double-close; vectorstore close() on a shared client; corpus_hash param mis-binding the determinism record; dynamic version build breakage; mypy-unpinned new errors on 3.12-3.14. Use git log/diff of pass 1 + PoCs.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

-def scrubbed_env() -> dict[str, str]:
-    """The process env with every metered Anthropic credential removed.
+# Env vars that — beyond metered credentials — would redirect the subprocess OFF-BOX or onto a
+# metered provider, defeating ADR-0003 / "no metered API". The claude CLI honours all of these.
+_UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS = frozenset(
+    {
+        "ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL",
+        "ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL",
+        "ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL",
+        "CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK",
+        "CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX",
+        "CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER",
+        "GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS",
+    }
+)
+
+
+def is_unsafe_subprocess_var(key: str) -> bool:
+    """True for any env var that must not reach the high-stakes subprocess (#101).
+
+    Covers metered credentials, off-box/metered-provider redirects, proxies (which redirect
+    egress), and cloud-provider credentials (AWS_*/GOOGLE_*). The subscription OAuth token is
+    deliberately NOT matched.
+    """
+    ku = key.upper()
+    return (
+        is_metered_anthropic_credential(key)
+        or ku in _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS
+        or ku.endswith("_PROXY")  # HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY/NO_PROXY (any case)
+        or ku.startswith("AWS_")
+        or ku.startswith("GOOGLE_")
+    )

-    present, would silently bill per token — so strip it before the SDK subprocess sees it.
+
+def scrubbed_env() -> dict[str, str]:
+    """The process env hardened for the high-stakes subprocess (ADR-0003 / no metered API).
+
+    Every unsafe var (:func:`is_unsafe_subprocess_var`) is **overwritten to empty**, not merely
+    omitted. The claude-agent-sdk builds the child env as ``{**os.environ, **options.env}`` — it
+    *merges* over the inherited environment — so a dropped key would silently fall through to its
+    inherited value (the metered key / off-box redirect would survive). Setting it to ``""`` in
+    the dict we hand over guarantees the merge overwrites the inherited value (#101). The
+    subscription OAuth token is preserved so the fixed-cost path still authenticates.
     """
-    return {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if not is_metered_anthropic_credential(k)}
+    env = dict(os.environ)
+    for k in list(env):
+        if is_unsafe_subprocess_var(k):
+            env[k] = ""
+    return env

@@ -150,13 +195,20 @@ def _submit_bounded(fn: Callable[[], Any]) -> Any:
     global _inflight
     with _inflight_lock:
         if _inflight >= _POOL_WORKERS:
-            raise PermanentReasoningError(
+            # Saturation is a TRANSIENT condition — surface a retryable backpressure signal so
+            # _run_with_retries waits and re-tries rather than failing the caller permanently
+            # (#104). Concurrent callers thus queue briefly instead of erroring out.
+            raise RateLimitError(

-                "hung backend call(s) have leaked every worker slot"
+                "backpressure; retry shortly"

+    except RuntimeError as exc:  # pool shut down at interpreter exit — normalize, don't leak (#116)
+        with _inflight_lock:

@@ -438,8 +490,26 @@ def _attempt(request: ReasoningRequest) -> str:

-def _run_with_retries(request: ReasoningRequest) -> str:
-    """Execute with independent transient and rate-limit retry budgets."""
+def _sleep_bounded(secs: float, deadline: float | None) -> None:
+    """Sleep ``secs``, but never past ``deadline`` — so backoff can't blow an overall budget.
+
+    If the deadline has already passed, raise instead of sleeping the full interval, so a long

So the proxy strip was intentional and considered. The pass-1 reasoning: the high-stakes prompt is the full analysis content, and a proxy would redirect egress off-box. So stripping `*_PROXY` is by design to prevent exfiltration. This is a documented hard constraint, so the proxy strip is NOT a regression to report (it would block legitimate corporate-proxy use, but that's an accepted tradeoff per their threat model). I'll note it but not as a primary finding.

Now let me look at a real subtle regression: `is_unsafe_subprocess_var` uses `ku.endswith("_PROXY")`. This matches `NO_PROXY` too. But also it would match any var ending in `_PROXY`. More importantly — `ku.startswith("GOOGLE_")` AND the `_UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS` set contains `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` which is redundant (already matched by `GOOGLE_` prefix). Minor.

Let me now look at the other flagged files. Let me check provenance (lock), vectorstore (close on shared client), checkpoint (pg pool double-close), versioning (corpus_hash), pipeline.

content.text:
The lock is a standard non-reentrant `threading.Lock`. The `record` validates `kind` outside the lock, then calls `_record_locked` under the lock. `as_recorder()`'s `_record` calls `self.record(...)` — let me verify it doesn't call `_record_locked` while holding the lock (reentrancy → deadlock). It calls `self.record`, which acquires the lock fresh, so no nested acquire. Good, no deadlock there.

But is there a path where `record` is called while the lock is held? The clock callback is invoked inside `_record_locked` while holding the lock — if an injected clock calls back into `record`, it would deadlock. That's an exotic injected-dependency scenario; not a realistic regression.

Now the prevhash chain. The lock makes append atomic. But note: `as_recorder` is used by gate nodes. Let me check the make_gate_node recorder bridge for potential deadlock if it records while a graph holds something. Let me look at the gatenode/graph diff.

content.text:
Here's a real regression concern. The recorder is called AFTER `interrupt()` returns and the decision is validated. But in LangGraph, when a node resumes after an interrupt, the node function re-executes from the BEGINNING (interrupt replays the whole node, not just resumes at the interrupt point). Wait, actually `interrupt()` in LangGraph: when the graph is resumed, the node is re-invoked from the start, and `interrupt()` returns the resume value instead of pausing. So the code before `interrupt()` (proposal computation) runs again, but that's idempotent here.

The recorder call at line 131 happens once per successful decision. But here's the subtle issue: if the recorder itself raises (e.g. provenance serialization error), the node raises AFTER `interrupt` already consumed the resume value, and the validated decision is lost — but the gate state update is also lost (the return never happens). On the next resume, `already_decided` returns False (no decision recorded in state), so it re-fires interrupt. Actually this is acceptable degradation.

A bigger concern: the recorder bridges to `trail.record`, which is now lock-guarded. If the graph runs nodes concurrently (LangGraph can run parallel branches) and the same trail's lock is contended, that's fine (it's a lock, designed for it). No deadlock.

Let me now focus on the items most likely to be genuine regressions. Let me check the versioning corpus_hash and pyproject dynamic version, and the determinism.py changes.

Now I've found several genuine regressions. Let me analyze the most significant ones:

**1. `path_for` regex uses `fullmatch` with `[0-9a-f]{64}` — but `put()` returns `hashlib.sha256().hexdigest()` which is lowercase hex. That's fine. But `re.compile(r"[0-9a-f]{64}")` without anchors used with `.fullmatch()` is fine. Not a bug.**

**2. The pipeline `run_trace_context` / `ExitStack` placement — CRITICAL regression candidate.** Look: `trace_ctx = ExitStack()` and `trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id))` are created BEFORE the `try:` block. If `run_trace_context(corr_id)` raises during `enter_context`, the ExitStack is never closed (but it's empty, so fine). The bigger issue: the `finally:` closes `trace_ctx`. But what if `bootstrap_tracing()` or `enter_context` raises? Then we never enter the try and `tracker` is left started without `end_run`. Let me check whether bootstrap is best-effort. Let me verify the `run_trace_context` and the GateReview freeze regression more carefully.

Let me check the GateReview - the `freeze_mapping` on payload. GateReview is used as a handler argument. If payload now is frozen, does any handler mutate it or rely on it being a plain dict?

1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
62	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
63	
64	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
65	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
66	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
67	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
68	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
69	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
70	    """
71	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
72	        return value
73	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
74	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
75	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
76	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
77	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
78	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
79	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
80	        return bytes(value)
81	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
82	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)

This is the **corpus_hash mis-binding regression** the prompt flagged. The function trusts `corpus_hash` blindly — it's used both as the recorded `corpus_fingerprint` AND folded into the seed derivation. If a caller passes a stale/wrong hash, the entire determinism record (fingerprint + derived seed) is corrupted, and `corpus` is then NOT hashed at all (so the corpus content is never actually bound to the record). For a "determinism harness" whose entire purpose is reproducibility provenance, accepting an unverified corpus_hash silently defeats the guarantee.

The pipeline happens to re-validate (line 356), but `build_baseline_artifact` is a public-ish builder primitive. A standalone builder passing a wrong/typo'd corpus_hash gets a record that lies about which corpus it describes — with no error. This is a real, if MEDIUM, regression: the determinism record can be silently decoupled from the data. The fix should be cheap to make safe: if both `corpus` and `corpus_hash` are supplied, verify they agree (or document that the param is trusted and have the pipeline pass it only after computing it).

I've completed a thorough scrutiny of all 22 pass-1 changes. Let me compile my findings. The genuine regressions introduced by pass-1 fixes:

1. **GateReview payload deep-freeze breaks JSON serialization (public handler API)** — confirmed PoC, MEDIUM/HIGH
2. **corpus_hash accepted unverified → determinism record decoupled from corpus** — confirmed PoC, MEDIUM
3. **Pipeline tracing bootstrap outside the try → tracker run leak on tracing failure** — structural, LOW
4. **scrubbed_env wipes *_PROXY breaking proxied operation** — documented tradeoff, LOW

The other 18 fixes (scrubbed_env overwrite logic, run_json deadline/reroll, provenance lock, pg-pool close, vectorstore close, version dynamic, mypy unpin, fence keys, upsert batching, etc.) are clean — verified by build, full test suite (407 pass), mypy --strict clean on 3.14, and targeted reads/PoCs.

findings.title: GateReview payload deep-freeze (fix #113) breaks JSON serialization of the public handler payload
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #113 added GateReview.__post_init__ that replaces the payload Mapping with a deep-frozen FrozenDict (object.__setattr__('payload', freeze_mapping(self.payload))). GateReview is a PUBLIC, operator-facing extension type (exported from assay_engine/__init__.py:46,81; GateHandler = Callable[[GateReview], GateDecision] is the documented operator-review extension point). Before the fix, review.payload was a plain dict; now it is a FrozenDict, which is NOT JSON-serializable. Any custom GateHandler that does the most natural thing — serialize/log the payload for an operator-review UI (json.dumps(review.payload)) — now raises TypeError. Confirmed PoC: GateReview(...,payload={'hypothesis_ids':['h1','h2'],'meta':{'n':3}}) -> json.dumps(r.payload) raises 'TypeError: Object of type FrozenDict is not JSON serializable'. Secondary: freeze() raises TypeError at construction for any non-hashable leaf, so payloads that previously constructed fine (e.g. containing an arbitrary non-hashable object) now fail at GateReview(...) construction. This is a behavioral regression in a public API surface, introduced solely to make the record hashable.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep immutability without breaking JSON: either (a) freeze to a structure json.dumps handles (e.g. keep a plain immutable dict via MappingProxyType plus a separate hashing path), or (b) give FrozenDict a JSON path (register or expose a to_dict()/__json__ and document that handlers must call dict(review.payload) before json.dumps), or (c) document loudly in the GateHandler contract that payload is a FrozenDict and provide a convenience like review.payload_dict(). At minimum document the type change in the public API/changelog.

findings.title: build_baseline_artifact accepts an unverified corpus_hash (fix #119), silently decoupling the determinism record from the corpus
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #119 added a corpus_hash parameter so a caller can pass a precomputed fingerprint and skip recomputing it. The function trusts the value blindly: 'corpus_hash = corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)'. When supplied, the corpus argument is NEVER actually hashed — the passed string is recorded as BaselineArtifact.corpus_fingerprint AND folded into input_hashes['corpus'] which drives the deterministic seed (stable_seed). A stale/typo'd/mismatched corpus_hash therefore produces a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived seed describe a DIFFERENT corpus than the one passed, with no error. For a determinism/reproducibility harness this silently defeats the core guarantee (the record no longer binds the data it claims to). Confirmed PoC: build_baseline_artifact(corpus, contents=..., corpus_hash='deadbeef'*8) returns corpus_fingerprint='deadbeef...' and a different seed than the honest build, while corpus_fingerprint(corpus) is a completely different value. The pipeline happens to re-validate downstream (pipeline.py:356 raises on baseline.corpus_fingerprint != cfp), but build_baseline_artifact is a builder primitive usable standalone, where nothing catches the mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: When BOTH corpus and corpus_hash are provided, verify they agree: compute corpus_fingerprint(corpus) lazily only if needed, or assert corpus_hash == corpus_fingerprint(corpus) (cheap relative to the build) and raise ValueError on mismatch. Alternatively make corpus and corpus_hash mutually exclusive, or document that corpus_hash is fully trusted and is the caller's integrity responsibility.

findings.title: Tracing bootstrap/context entered outside the try (fix #122) can leak a started experiment-tracker run
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #122 inserted bootstrap_tracing() and trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id)) AFTER tracker.start_run() (line 271) but BEFORE the main 'try:' (line 288) whose 'finally' is the only place tracker.end_run() runs (line 559-562). If either bootstrap_tracing() or enter_context(run_trace_context(...)) raises, the tracker run was already started but the finally is never reached, so the MLflow run leaks in RUNNING state forever (the very class of bug #110 fixed), and the ExitStack/attached baggage context may also leak. Both tracing helpers are written best-effort (they swallow exceptions internally — bootstrap_tracing wraps everything, run_trace_context only acts after a successful import), so the probability is low, but the ordering is structurally fragile: any future change that lets these raise reintroduces an unended-run leak.
findings.suggested_fix: Move bootstrap_tracing() and trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id)) inside the existing try block (after the try:, before phase INGEST), so the finally's tracker.end_run cleanup covers a tracing-setup failure; or wrap them in their own try/except that records a tracking_error and proceeds untraced.

findings.title: scrubbed_env wipes all *_PROXY vars (fix #101), breaking the subscription call behind a corporate proxy
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #101 extends scrubbing to overwrite HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY/NO_PROXY (any-case '_PROXY' suffix), AWS_*, GOOGLE_*, and provider-redirect vars to empty string in the subprocess env. The high-stakes tier's Claude CLI subprocess makes an inherently REMOTE call to the Anthropic subscription endpoint (loopback-only is a DATA constraint per ADR-0003, not the LLM path). On an operator network that reaches the internet only via an egress proxy, wiping HTTPS_PROXY (and NO_PROXY) to '' makes the subscription auth/generation call fail outright — legitimate operation breaks. Confirmed: scrubbed_env() returns HTTPS_PROXY -> ''. This was a deliberate decision (PASS-1.md documents proxies as an exfiltration/SSRF channel for the full analysis prompt), so it is a designed tradeoff rather than an accidental defect — but it is an operability regression worth an explicit escape hatch.
findings.suggested_fix: If proxy stripping must stay for the threat model, document it prominently as a hard requirement (on-box direct egress to api.anthropic.com required) and/or provide an opt-in allowlist env (e.g. ASSAY_ALLOW_PROXY) that, when set, preserves a validated proxy var so proxied operators are not silently broken.

I completed an adversarial scrutiny of all 22 pass-1 fixes (commit `1389242`) for regressions, backed by build, full test suite (407 passed/5 skipped), mypy --strict (clean on 3.14), and targeted PoCs. Four genuine regressions found:

**HIGH — GateReview payload deep-freeze breaks JSON (#113 regression)**
`src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:90-92`. `__post_init__` now replaces the public, handler-facing `payload` with a `FrozenDict`. `GateReview`/`GateHandler` are exported public API. PoC: `json.dumps(review.payload)` now raises `TypeError: Object of type FrozenDict is not JSON serializable` — breaking any operator handler that logs/serializes the review payload. Also raises at construction for previously-acceptable non-hashable leaves.

**MEDIUM — corpus_hash accepted unverified (#119 regression)**
`src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:106-124`. The new `corpus_hash` param is trusted blindly; when supplied, `corpus` is never hashed, and the bad hash is recorded as the fingerprint AND folded into the derived seed. PoC: a wrong `corpus_hash` yields a determinism record whose fingerprint+seed describe a different corpus, with no error — silently defeating the reproducibility guarantee for standalone builder use (the pipeline re-validates at line 356, but the primitive does not).

**LOW — tracing bootstrap outside the try (#122 regression)**
`src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:283-288`. `bootstrap_tracing()` and `enter_context(run_trace_context(...))` run after `tracker.start_run()` but before the `try:` whose `finally` calls `end_run`. If either raises, the tracker run leaks RUNNING forever (the exact class #110 fixed). Low probability today because both helpers are best-effort, but structurally fragile.

**LOW — scrubbed_env wipes *_PROXY (#101 tradeoff)**
`src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:143-174`. Wiping `HTTPS_PROXY`/`NO_PROXY` to `""` breaks the inherently-remote subscription call behind a corporate egress proxy. Documented as a deliberate exfiltration-channel defense in PASS-1.md, so a designed tradeoff — flagged for an explicit escape hatch/doc.

The remaining 18 fixes are clean and verified: `scrubbed_env` overwrite-to-empty correctly preserves OAuth/PATH/HOME; `run_json` deadline/parse-only-reroll has no multiplication or edge-case regression and no internal callers depended on old behavior; provenance lock is non-reentrant with no deadlock path and preserves determinism; pg-pool close-on-failure is idempotent and de-registered (no double-close); vectorstore `close()` only closes an owned client; `upsert` batching is length-guarded; dynamic version builds correctly (`assay_engine-0.0.1` wheel) and is single-sourced; mypy unpin is clean on 3.14 with no PEP-695/3.12-only syntax in the tree; fence key-scanning is correct.
